## google's map api

June 29, 2005   

---

[Adrian](http://web.archive.org/web/20110215085610/http://www.holovaty.com/images/2005-06-18%5Ftribune%5Farticle.jpg)pointed out at the last [chipy](http://chipy.org) meeting that a formal API for GoogleMaps was in the works…but I had no idea it was [this](http://www.google.com/apis/maps/) close.

After you’ve got an authentication key for your site directory, all you need to do to embed a map in your page is include a javascript library source URL directly from google, create a <div> tag with an id (say “map”) and add some javascript to your page.

    var map = new GMap( document.getElementById("map") );
    map.addControl( new GSmallMapControl() );
    map.centerAndZoom( new GPoint(-88.316385,42.247090), 4);                                                                    

This took[literally](http://web.archive.org/web/20061012034456/http://www.inkdroid.org/google.html)2 minutes to do, if that. It’s a bit tedious that the token is only good on a per-directory basis, but I guess this is because of partitioned blogging sites where different users have different directories with the same hostname.

_update: I guess I’m not the only one who finds the per-directory limit to be kind of a[hassle](http://groups-beta.google.com/group/Google-Maps-API/browse%5Fthread/thread/a70d545eeed46d40/c7e8b0ad540232ae#c7e8b0ad540232ae)._

## lightning strikes

June 10, 2005   

---

Chris has a nice[writeup](http://weblog.lonelylion.com/?p=512) about last nights ChiPy lightning talks. There were tons of interesting people there with very interesting projects. Apart from the announcement that we might be hosting [PyCon](http://www.python.org/pycon/)next year in Chicago, the highlight of the evening for me was hearing about the[amazing](http://web.archive.org/web/20050707011548/http://www.cnn.com:80/2005/TECH/internet/06/09/google.map.hacks.ap/)data hack that is[ChicagoCrime](http://www.chicagocrime.org/).[Adrian](http://www.holovaty.com/) is a journalist/programmer who managed to glue together GoogleMaps with publicly available data from the Chicago Police Department. The main (perhaps unintended) things I took from his enthusiastic and humorous talk were:

* screen scraping is fragile but it’s an important lever for fostering more elegant/robust information sharing.
* screen scraping is fragile but it’s important for building new_public_ applications that aren’t run behind closed doors at the Department of Homeland Security

I really, really want to get going on the GovTrack data scraping now.

## govtrack

June 9, 2005   

---

[GovTrack](http://www.govtrack.us) has done some awesome work generating publicly available[machine readable data](http://web.archive.org/web/20080517173534/http://www.govtrack.us/source.xpd) for US government information. After the last election I decided that I really wanted to get involved in some sort of volunteer technology/political activity, so I started googling and found GovTrack pretty much just starting up. Now there is a loose affiliation of similar sites (including GovTrack) called[Ogdex](http://www.ogdex.com/) who are attempting to foster the collection of publicly available government information. In particular there has been some[talk](http://groups.yahoo.com/group/govtrack/message/138) on the govtrack discussion list about local efforts to add state data to the collection of federal data…and even bounties for getting state data collection going. I’m going to take a stab at writing some scraping utilities for gathering together Illinois data and will report back with how it goes. If you are interested in helping out details are[available](http://www.ogdex.com/warehouse/space/Wish+List).

_Update_: Joshua just set up a new drupal[site](http://web.archive.org/web/20060220120807/http://www.govtrack.us:80/dev/)for govtrack development.

## pybibutils

June 8, 2005   

---

The #code4lib sprint is coming up soon and (alas) we still don’t really have a firm grasp on what we will be sprinting on. After pycon dchud had some ideas for a metadata wrangling framework for python. Around the same time I was working on [SWIG](http://www.swig.org/)wrapper for the[bibutils](http://www.scripps.edu/~cdputnam/software/bibutils/bibutils.html)library. So one idea we had was to create this python utility that would enable converting between many of the popular metadata/citation formats:

* [MARC](http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/)
* [MODS](http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/)
* [BibTex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BibTeX)
* [EndNote](http://www.endnote.com/)
* [RIS](http://www.refman.com/)
* [ISI](http://www.isinet.com/)

Emerging details are available on the[wiki](http://web.archive.org/web/20050827220707/http://wiki.inkdroid.org:80/code4lib/pybibutils). If you have _any_ ideas for the sprint please note them on the[wiki](http://wiki.inkdroid.org/code4lib).

## one billion

May 16, 2005   
[oclc-worldcat](https://inkdroid.org/2005/05/16/oclc/tag/oclc-worldcat) 

---

[Thom Hickey](http://outgoing.typepad.com/outgoing/2005/05/%5Fof%5Frecords%5Fin%5F.html) mentioned a new[page](http://www.oclc.org/worldcat/grow.htm) at OCLC which lists some real time stats for worldcat: total holdings, last record added, etc. Perhaps this is in honor of the total holdings getting very close to crossing the 1 billion mark.

So of course I had to add a plugin for panizzi to scrape the page. Rather than writing yet another state machine for parsing html I decided to try out Frederik Lundh’s[ElementTree Tidy HTML Tree Builder](http://effbot.org/zone/element-tidylib.htm), which works out very well when you want to walk a datastructure representing possibly invalid HTML.

    url = "http://www.oclc.org/worldcat/grow.htm"
    tree = TidyHTMLTreeBuilder.parse( urlopen( self.url ) )

That’s all there is to getting nice elementtree object which you can dig into for a page of HTML.

So, predictably:

10:53 < edsu> @worldcat
10:53 < panizzi> edsu: [May 16, 2005 11:49 AM EDT #981,277,234] 
                      El senor de los anillos. Tolkien, J. R. R. ... 
                      uploaded by OEL - EUGENE PUB LIBR

one billion

May 16, 2005
oclc-worldcat

Thom Hickey mentioned a new page at OCLC which lists some real time stats for worldcat: total holdings, last record added, etc. Perhaps this is in honor of the total holdings getting very close to crossing the 1 billion mark.

So of course I had to add a plugin for panizzi to scrape the page. Rather than writing yet another state machine for parsing html I decided to try out Frederik Lundh’s ElementTree Tidy HTML Tree Builder, which works out very well when you want to walk a datastructure representing possibly invalid HTML. 

    url = "http://www.oclc.org/worldcat/grow.htm"
    tree = TidyHTMLTreeBuilder.parse( urlopen( self.url ) )

That’s all there is to getting nice elementtree object which you can dig into for a page of HTML.

So, predictably: 

10:53 < edsu> @worldcat
10:53 < panizzi> edsu: [May 16, 2005 11:49 AM EDT #981,277,234] 
                      El senor de los anillos. Tolkien, J. R. R. ... 
                      uploaded by OEL - EUGENE PUB LIBR
